title: Client Report - Exploring Names
text:
Show the code
import polars as pl
import numpy as np
from lets_plot import *

LetsPlot.setup_html(isolated_frame=True)
Show the code
# Learn morea about Code Cells: https://quarto.org/docs/reference/cells/cells-jupyter.html

# Include and execute your code here
df = pl.read_csv("https://posit.byui.edu/content/0d43d66f-d3b7-4c43-8c36-52e3ed69e7f1/names_year_csv.csv")

title: Client Report - Exploring Names
text:
QUESTION 1
What was the earliest year that the name ‘Felisha’ was used?
The earliest year Felisha was used was 1964. It was used 20 times, 9 times in Texas alone. We can find this by filtering the name and sorting by the year.


Show the code
# Q1
print(df.filter(name = "Felisha").sort('year').head(1))

title: Client Report - Exploring Names
text:
QUESTION 2
What year had the most babies named ‘David’? How many babies were named ‘David’ that year?
This data captures 114 years that the name David was used. It was used with the most frequency in the year 1955, when there were 86,389 people named David.


Show the code
# Q2
print(df.filter(name = "David").sort('Total', descending=True).head(1))

title: Client Report - Exploring Names
text:
QUESTION 3
What year did your name hit its peak? How many babies were named your name in that year?
My name, Ella, hit it’s peak in 2010, being used 9,883 times.


Show the code
# Q3
print(df.filter(name = "Ella").sort("Total", descending=True).head(1))

title: Client Report - Exploring Names
text:
QUESTION 4
In the most recent year, what was the most common female name in Utah?
Here we see the most common female name in Utah in the year 2024 is Olivia. Here we are assuming the names Oliver, Liam, Henry, and William are all male names. This assumption leaves us with Olivia, which was used 215 times in the state of Utah in the year 2024.


Show the code
# Q5
dfq4 = df.select (
  pl.col ('name'),
  pl.col("year"),
  pl.col("UT"),
)
dfq4 = dfq4.sort("year", "UT", descending = True)
print(dfq4.head())

title: Client Report - Flights: Creating New Columns
text:
Show the code
import polars as pl
import requests #new for reading in JSON
import io       #new for reading in JSON
import numpy as np
from lets_plot import *

LetsPlot.setup_html(isolated_frame=True)
Show the code
# Learn morea about Code Cells: https://quarto.org/docs/reference/cells/cells-jupyter.html

# Include and execute your code here
url = "https://raw.githubusercontent.com/byuidatascience/data4missing/master/data-raw/flights_missing/flights_missing.json"
response = requests.get(url)
df = pl.read_json(io.BytesIO(response.content))

title: Client Report - Flights: Creating New Columns
text:
QUESTION 1

Create a new ‘season’ column. Define ‘season’ as:

Winter for January, Febuary (note the mispelling), March
Spring for April, May, June
Summer for July, August, September
Fall for October, November, December
All other rows can be assigned a value of ‘Unknown’


No discussion needed for this question


Show the code
# Include and execute your code here

df = df.with_columns(season = pl.when(pl.col("month").is_in(["January", "Febuary", "March"])).then(pl.lit("Winter")).when(pl.col("month").is_in(["April", "May", "June"])).then(pl.lit("Spring")).when(pl.col("month").is_in(["July", "August", "September"])).then(pl.lit("Summer")).when(pl.col("month").is_in(["October", "November", "December"])).then(pl.lit("Fall")).otherwise(pl.lit("Unknown")))
print(df.select("month", "season").head(5))

title: Client Report - Flights: Creating New Columns
text:
QUESTION 2

Divide all the columns by 1000 whose name starts with num_of. Be sure to first filter out rows that contain problematic values for the columns like -999 or 1500+.

No discussion needed for this question


Show the code
# Include and execute your code here

df = (df.with_columns(pl.selectors.starts_with("num_of").cast(pl.Int64, strict=False)).filter(~pl.any_horizontal(pl.selectors.starts_with("num_of") == -999)).with_columns(pl.selectors.starts_with("num_of") / 1000))
print(df.select("month", "num_of_delays_weather", "num_of_delays_late_aircraft", "num_of_delays_nas").head(5))

title: Client Report - Flights: Creating New Columns
text:
QUESTION 3

According to the BTS website, the “Weather” category only accounts for severe weather delays. Mild weather delays are not counted in the “Weather” category, but are actually included in both the “nas” and “late_aircraft” categories. Using the columns you modified in the bullet above, your job is to create a new column that calculates the total number of flights (in 1,000’s) delayed by weather (both severe and mild). Show your work by printing the first 5 rows of the dataset. Use these three rules for your calculations:

100% of delayed flights in the Weather category are due to weather

30% of all delayed flights in the “late_aircraft” category are due to weather

From April through August, 40% of delayed flights in the NAS category are due to weather. The rest of the months, the proportion rises to 65%


No discussion needed for this question


Show the code
# Include and execute your code here
#df = df.with_columns(weather = (pl.col("num_of_delays_weather") + (pl.col("num_of_delays_late_aircraft") * .3) + pl.when(pl.col("month").is_in(["April", "May", "June", "July", "August"])).then(pl.col("num_of_delays_nas") * .4).otherwise(pl.col("num_of_delays_nas") * .65) ))
df = df.with_columns(
    total_weather_delays = (
        pl.col("num_of_delays_weather")
        + (pl.col("num_of_delays_late_aircraft") * 0.30)
        + pl.when(pl.col("month").is_in(["April", "May", "June", "July", "August"]))
            .then(pl.col("num_of_delays_nas") * 0.40)
            .otherwise(pl.col("num_of_delays_nas")* 0.65)
    )
)
print(df.select("month", "num_of_delays_weather", "num_of_delays_late_aircraft", "num_of_delays_nas", "total_weather_delays").head(5))

title: Client Report - Flights: Creating New Columns
text:
QUESTION 4

Using the new weather variable calculated above, create a boxplot showing the distribution of flights delayed by weather for each season. Describe what you learn from this graph.

Here the graph shows us the results we were mainly expecting: in the winter there are more flight delays than any other season. We find that the spread of “Unknown” seasons is very wide, which checks out because it could be they delay of any given season reported here.


Show the code
# Include and execute your code here
(ggplot(df, aes(x="season", y="total_weather_delays", color = "season"))
    + geom_boxplot()
    + labs(title = "Quantity of Weather Delays vs Season", x = "Season", y = "Quantity of Weather Delays")
)

title: Client Report - Can You Predict That?
text:
Show the code
import polars as pl
import numpy as np
from lets_plot import *
# add the additional libraries you need to import for ML here
from sklearn.model_selection import train_test_split
from sklearn import metrics
from sklearn.ensemble import RandomForestClassifier
LetsPlot.setup_html(isolated_frame=True)
Show the code
# import your data here using polars and the local dataset file
data_path = 'dwellings_ml.txt'
dwellings = pl.read_csv(data_path)

title: Client Report - Can You Predict That?
text:
QUESTION

Take time to get to know your data. Based on some initial exploration, identify 3 variables in the dataset you think will have strong predictive ability for determining if a house was built before 1980. Explain/justify the reasoning behind your guesses (for example with plots).

The three variables I think would be helpful is the living area, finished basement, and condition of the house. These don’t have a huge correlation, but I think all together they could be helpful.


Show the code
# Optional: code you used to get to know your data

print('Dataset shape:', dwellings.shape)
for c in dwellings.columns:
    print(c)
print(dwellings.select(pl.col("yrbuilt", "stories", "before1980")))

plot_data = dwellings.select(['yrbuilt', 'livearea', 'finbsmnt', 'condition_AVG']).to_pandas()

p1 = (
    ggplot(plot_data, aes(x='yrbuilt', y='livearea'))
    + geom_point(alpha=0.2, size=2)
    + labs( title = 'Year Built vs Living Area', x = 'Year Built', y = 'Living Area')
    + scale_x_continuous(format='{d}')
)

p2 = (
    ggplot(plot_data, aes(x='yrbuilt', y='finbsmnt'))
    + geom_point(alpha=0.2, size=2)
    + labs( title = 'Year Built vs Finished Basement', x = 'Year Built', y = 'Finished Basement')
    + scale_x_continuous(format='{d}')
)

p3 = (
    ggplot(plot_data, aes(x='yrbuilt'))
    + geom_bar()
    + labs( title = 'Count of Houses by Year Built', x = 'Year Built', y = 'Count')
    + scale_x_continuous(format='{d}')
)

plot_grid = gggrid([p1, p2, p3], ncol=1)
plot_grid



Build a classification model labeling houses as being built “before 1980” or “during or after 1980”. Your goal is to reach or exceed 90% accuracy. Report your final model choice and any other model parameters you may have tweaked (train-test split ratio, tuning parameters, etc).

Here we’re able to use RandomForestClassifier to predict whether a house was built before 1980. I used few features at first and then increased the number of features to see if it would increase the accuracy – it did, by about 3%. I tweaked the train-test ratio to 70-30 and that seemed to work well, but I didn’t do any tuning of the model parameters. I think that could be a next step to try to increase the accuracy even more.


Show the code
# Include and execute your code here
# Don't forget, you need another import statement to import the desired ML model from sklearn
features = dwellings.select(['livearea','finbsmnt','basement','stories','nocars','numbdrm','numbaths','condition_AVG',
    'quality_A','quality_B','quality_C','quality_D','quality_X',
    'gartype_Att','gartype_Det','gartype_None','gartype_CP']).to_numpy()
target = dwellings.select('before1980').to_numpy().ravel()

x_train, x_test, y_train, y_test = train_test_split(features, target, test_size=0.3, stratify=target)

model = RandomForestClassifier()
model.fit(x_train, y_train)

y_pred = model.predict(x_test)

acc = metrics.accuracy_score(y_test, y_pred)
print('accuracy:', acc)

title: Client Report - Star Wars for Dummies
text:
Show the code
import polars as pl 
import numpy as np
from lets_plot import *
import polars.selectors as cs
import pandas as pd
# imports for ML
from sklearn.model_selection import train_test_split
from sklearn import metrics
from sklearn.ensemble import RandomForestClassifier
from sklearn.metrics import classification_report

LetsPlot.setup_html(isolated_frame=True)

data_url = "https://byuidatascience.github.io/DS250-Course-Polars/Data/StarWars_with_emails.csv"
Show the code
# Read in and tidy up the first row
a = pl.Series(pl.read_csv(data_url, skip_rows = 10, n_rows=1, has_header=False, encoding = 'utf8-lossy').row(0))
# Note the non-aschii character in the column "Do you consider yourself to be a fan of the Expanded Universe". (column AF if you open it in Excel). 

# BTW: Detecting what it is and where can be difficult

# Lossy means that invalid utf8 values are replaced with � characters. When using other encodings than utf8 or utf8-lossy, the input is first decoded in memory with python. Defaults to utf8...ignore_errors = True does not replace the non-utf8 character with a blank space like you would hope

a[2] = "seen_any" #simply overwrite the value at index 2

# Rather than changing each column header manually,
# here we use regex to apply changes more efficiently
a = (
  a.str.replace(r".+seen.+", "seen_")
   .str.replace(r".+rank.+", "rank_")
   .str.replace(r"(.+favorably.+)", "fav_")
   .fill_null(strategy = "forward")

   .str.replace("\uFFFD+", "") #this gets rid of the question marks in a diamond characters 
   .str.strip_chars()
   .str.to_lowercase()
   .str.replace_all(" ", "_")
   .str.replace(r"location(.+)", "location")

)
a[3] = "star_wars_fan"
a[30] = "who_shot_first"
a[31] = "know_expanded_universe"
a[32] = "expanded_universe_fan"
a[33] = "star_trek_fan"
Show the code
b = pl.Series(pl.read_csv(data_url, skip_rows = 11, n_rows=1, has_header=False, encoding = 'utf8-lossy').row(0))

b = (
  b.str.replace(r"Star Wars: (Episode \w+) .+", "${1}")
   .str.replace("Response", "")
   .str.replace_all(" ", "_")
   .fill_null("")
   .str.strip_chars()
)
Show the code
#Now combine the two series, each of which contains part of the desired column header
new_names = (a + b).to_list()

# Read in the data, skipping over the column header rows
# and use 'new_names' as the column headers
df = pl.read_csv(data_url, skip_rows=12, has_header=False, new_columns = new_names )

title: Client Report - Star Wars for Dummies
text:
Executive Summary

This report uses the FiveThirtyEight Star Wars survey to predict whether a respondent’s household income is above $50,000 using a Random Forest Classifier.
The dataset includes 835 respondents who have seen at least one Star Wars film. Survey responses were cleaned and encoded for use in the model.
The model achieved approximately 65% accuracy on the test set.
The stretch was completed: two charts from the article are recreated and validate the dataset.

title: Client Report - Star Wars for Dummies
text:
Methods
Regex is used to parse the email column and extract each respondent’s first name, last name, and email domain into separate columns. Each pattern uses markers in the email — such as the start of a string and the @ symbol — to isolate the relevant text.


Show the code
# Extract first name, last name, and email domain from the email column
df = df.with_columns([
    pl.col("email").str.strip_chars().str.extract(r"^([a-zA-Z]+)", 1).alias("first_name"),
    pl.col("email").str.strip_chars().str.extract(r"([a-zA-Z]+)@", 1).alias("last_name"),
    pl.col("email").str.strip_chars().str.extract(r"@([a-zA-Z0-9]+)", 1).alias("email_domain"),
])
df.select(["email", "first_name", "last_name", "email_domain"]).head(5)


Here we count the email domains, normalizing all domains to lowercase before grouping, since the raw data contains inconsistent capitalization. This ensures that variations of the same domain are counted together rather than treated as separate entries.


Show the code
# Unique email domain count
domain_count = (df.with_columns(pl.col("email_domain").str.to_lowercase()).group_by("email_domain").len().sort("len", descending=True))
domain_count


The dataset is filtered to respondents who have seen at least one Star Wars film. Rather than relying on the self-reported seen_any column, we check the six individual film columns directly — if any are non-null, the respondent has seen at least one film.


Show the code
# Respondents who have seen at least one Star Wars film
seen_cols = [c for c in df.columns if c.startswith("seen_") and c != "seen_any"]
df_seen = df.filter(
    pl.any_horizontal([pl.col(c).is_not_null() for c in seen_cols])
)

print(df_seen.head(5))


Here we pull apart the answers that are numbers yet currently formatted as strings and turn them into numbers. For range values, the lower number is used. The categories must be numeric for machine learning, so each category is mapped to an integer. This preserves the ordering of each variable and keeps the conversion simple and consistent. Rows where no income was reported are removed. For other missing values, we filled it with a 0 before passing to the model. Now instead of dropping important rows, we can just drop it from that feature. Now we can use ‘df_for_ml’ as our machine learning ready data set!


Show the code
# Age
age_map = {"18-29": 18, "30-44": 30, "45-60": 45, "> 60": 60}

df_for_ml = (
    df_seen
    .with_columns(pl.col("age").replace(age_map).cast(pl.Int32).alias("age_num"))
    .drop("age")
)
# Education
education_ = {
    "Less than high school degree": 1,
    "High school degree": 2,
    "Some college or Associate degree": 3,
    "Bachelor degree": 4,
    "Graduate degree": 5,
}

df_for_ml = (
    df_for_ml
    .with_columns(pl.col("education").replace(education_).cast(pl.Int32).alias("education_num"))
    .drop("education")
)
# Income
income = {
    "$0 - $24,999": 0,
    "$25,000 - $49,999": 25000,
    "$50,000 - $99,999": 50000,
    "$100,000 - $149,999": 100000,
    "$150,000+": 150000,
}

df_for_ml = (
    df_for_ml
    .with_columns(pl.col("household_income").replace(income).cast(pl.Int64).alias("income_num"))
    .drop("household_income")
)
# Favorability
fav = {
    "Very favorably": 5,
    "Somewhat favorably": 4,
    "Neither favorably nor unfavorably (neutral)": 3,
    "Somewhat unfavorably": 2,
    "Very unfavorably": 1,
    "Unfamiliar (N/A)": 0,
}

fav_columns = [c for c in df.columns if c.startswith("fav_")]

df_for_ml = (
    df_for_ml
    .with_columns([pl.col(c).replace(fav).cast(pl.Int32) for c in fav_columns])
)

# Drop identifier columns
df_for_ml = df_for_ml.drop(["respondentid", "first_name", "last_name", "email"])

# Household income 
df_for_ml = df_for_ml.with_columns(
    (pl.col("income_num") >= 50000).cast(pl.Int8).alias("high_income")
)

# show head of complete data set 
df_for_ml.select(["age_num", "education_num", "income_num"] + fav_columns).head(5)


Any remaining string inputs that couldn’t be mapped to an integer are one-hot encoded, converting them to binary columns. Here we’re able to create our Machine Learning model to predict if a respondent’s household income is over $50,000. We remove the rows where the respondents didn’t record their income.Then we choose our features and target. We then split the data 70/30 and created our model. The model’s accuracy is shown below.


Show the code
# One-hot encode remaining strings
str_cols = df_for_ml.select(cs.string()).columns
df_for_ml = df_for_ml.to_dummies(columns=str_cols)

# Remove rows where income was not reported
df_model = df_for_ml.filter(pl.col("high_income").is_not_null())

# Set features
feature_cols = [c for c in df_model.columns if c not in ["high_income", "income_num"]]

features = df_model.select(feature_cols).fill_null(0).to_numpy()
target = df_model.select("high_income").to_numpy().ravel()

x_train, x_test, y_train, y_test = train_test_split(features, target, test_size=0.3)

# Create model
model = RandomForestClassifier()
model.fit(x_train, y_train)

y_pred = model.predict(x_test)

acc = metrics.accuracy_score(y_test, y_pred)
print('accuracy:', acc)

title: Client Report - Star Wars for Dummies
text:
Results
Here is the shape of the table for all the respondents who have seen at least one film, adding up to a total of 835.


Show the code
# Shape of table for respondents who have seen at least one Star Wars film
print(df_seen.shape)


The model reached 65% accuracy on the test set. Looking at the classification report, precision and recall are stronger for the under $50k group than the over $50k group, which makes sense because higher-income respondents aren’t as represented in the data.


Show the code
print(classification_report(y_test, y_pred, target_names=["< $50k", ">= $50k"]))


To validate our dataset, we recreated two charts from the original FiveThirtyEight article. The distributions line up closely, confirming the data is accurate.


Show the code
# stretch — Chart 1: Which Star Wars films have you seen?
seen_film_cols = [c for c in df.columns if c.startswith("seen_") and c != "seen_any"]
n_seen = len(df_seen)

film_name_map = {
    "seen_Episode_I":   "The Phantom Menace",
    "seen_Episode_II":  "Attack of the Clones",
    "seen_Episode_III": "Revenge of the Sith",
    "seen_Episode_IV":  "A New Hope",
    "seen_Episode_V":   "The Empire Strikes Back",
    "seen_Episode_VI":  "Return of the Jedi",
}

episode_order = [
    "The Phantom Menace",
    "Attack of the Clones",
    "Revenge of the Sith",
    "A New Hope",
    "The Empire Strikes Back",
    "Return of the Jedi",
]

chart1 = pl.DataFrame([
    {"film": film_name_map[c],
     "pct": round(df_seen[c].is_not_null().sum() / n_seen * 100)}
    for c in seen_film_cols if c in film_name_map
])

# coord_flip reverses order, so feed the list bottom-to-top
film_order = list(reversed(episode_order))

(ggplot(chart1, aes(x="film", y="pct"))
    + geom_bar(stat="identity", fill="#008fd5", width=0.7)
    + geom_text(aes(label="pct"), nudge_y=3, format="{d}%", size=7, color="#008fd5")
    + coord_flip()
    + scale_x_discrete(limits=film_order)
    + scale_y_continuous(limits=[0, 100])
    + labs(
        title="Which 'Star Wars' Movies Have You Seen?",
        subtitle="Of 835 respondents who have seen any Star Wars film",
        x="", y="")
    + theme(
        panel_grid_major_y=element_blank(),
        panel_grid_minor=element_blank(),
        axis_text_y=element_text(hjust=1))
)




Show the code
# stretch — Chart 2 (article's Chart 5): Who Shot First?
shot_counts = (
    df_seen
    .filter(pl.col("who_shot_first").is_not_null())
    .group_by("who_shot_first")
    .len()
    .rename({"len": "count"})
    .with_columns(
        (pl.col("count") / pl.col("count").sum() * 100).round().alias("pct")
    )
)

n_answered = shot_counts["count"].sum()

# Order: Han on top, then Greedo, then "I don't understand..."
shot_order = ["I don't understand this question", "Greedo", "Han"]

(ggplot(shot_counts, aes(x="who_shot_first", y="pct"))
    + geom_bar(stat="identity", fill="#008fd5", width=0.7)
    + geom_text(aes(label="pct"), nudge_y=2, format="{d}%", size=7, color="#008fd5")
    + coord_flip()
    + scale_x_discrete(limits=shot_order)
    + scale_y_continuous(limits=[0, 60])
    + labs(
        title="Who Shot First?",
        subtitle=f"According to {n_answered} respondents",
        x="", y="")
    + theme(
        panel_grid_major_y=element_blank(),
        panel_grid_minor=element_blank(),
        axis_text_y=element_text(hjust=1))
)

title: Client Report - Star Wars for Dummies
text:
Conclusion
The model reached around 65% accuracy predicting whether a Star Wars fan earns over $50,000. The biggest limitation is that this is self-reported data, so people may not answer income questions accurately or at all. Going forward, it would be interesting to see if adding more demographic features or trying a different model type could push accuracy higher. The Star Wars data is fun to work with but probably isn’t the most reliable dataset for income prediction, but it is a good proof of concept that survey data can be used for this kind of prediction.

title: Isaac Newtons’s CV
text:
Physicist, Mathematician, Cambridge professor.

isaac@applesdofall.org | My wikipedia page



Standing on the shoulders of giants


Laws of motion, gravitation, minting coins, disliking Robert Hooke



Cooling, power series, optics, alchemy, planetary motions, apples.




1654-1660 The King’s School, Grantham.
June 1661 - now Trinity College, Cambridge

Sizar

1667 - death Trinity College, Cambridge

Fellow




2012 President, Royal Society, London, UK
Associate, French Academy of Science, Paris, France






1669 Newton Sir I, De analysi per æquationes numero terminorum infinitas.
1669 Lectiones opticæ.
etc. etc. etc.



2012 Infinitesimal calculus for solutions to physics problems, SMBC patent 001




1600 Royal Mint, London

Warden
Minted coins

1600 Lucasian professor of Mathematics, Cambridge University
